# MF2 Native Inflection And Term Forms

## Purpose

Define a small native inflection and grammar-agreement layer for Mojito MF2
without making MF2 itself a full grammar engine.

The target is controlled product text first: terms, nouns, noun phrases,
articles, count forms, and simple agreement. Translators should edit structured
term forms in Mojito; runtime callers should pass term IDs and explicit
dependencies, not concatenate English-shaped fragments.

## Recovered Prior Work

The old checkout path `/Users/ja/code/mojito-french-gender-exploration` is no
longer present on disk, but the useful work survived in the local Git branch
`ja/codex/french-gender-exploration` at commit
`02332a4fb745f24afb6298244ba389190f51439c`.

Relevant recovered artifacts on that branch:

- `dev-docs/experiments/french-gender/README.md`
- `dev-docs/experiments/french-gender/french_gender_experiment.py`
- `dev-docs/experiments/french-gender/results.md`
- `dev-docs/experiments/mf2-grammar-agreement/`

The French gender prototype used:

1. suffix rules;
2. a masculine-exception Bloom filter;
3. a feminine-exception Bloom filter;
4. an exact correction map for validation failures.

It was built from Lexique383. That prototype remains useful as an algorithmic
experiment, but the production direction should use a dictionary source with
clear redistribution terms. The reusable part is the architecture: dictionary
or explicit term lookup first, compact exception structures second, heuristics
only as fallback/prefill, and diagnostics for unknown words.

Recovered measurements:

| Profile | Nouns | Suffix-only | Exact after corrections | Compact estimate |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| `tiny-embed` | 2,000 | 49.25% | 100.00% | 2.4 KB |
| `web-medium` | 20,000 | 76.00% | 100.00% | 14.3 KB |
| `backend-large` | 42,295 | 78.82% | 100.00% | 24.9 KB |

The exact result applies only to the supported lexicon after build-time
validation. Unknown words remain best-effort.

## Heuristic Prototype Vs Unicode Dictionary

The recovered heuristic prototype and the Unicode dictionary solve different
parts of the problem:

| Axis | Recovered heuristic prototype | Unicode dictionary direction |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Source | Lexique383 experiment, not production-cleared here | `unicode-org/inflection` data under `Unicode-3.0` |
| First feature | French noun gender only | POS, gender, number, elision flags, inflection hooks |
| Runtime shape | Suffix rules + Bloom exceptions + correction map | Generated locale pack from dictionary entries |
| Exactness | Exact only for a declared validated lexicon | Exact for represented dictionary facts, still ambiguous by surface |
| Unknown words | Best-effort suffix/Bloom guess | Dictionary miss, then optional heuristic fallback |
| Product role | Prefill, fallback, and compact algorithm inspiration | Primary production data source after compilation |

The right combined approach is:

1. compile Unicode dictionary facts into the production locale pack;
2. derive compact exception/correction structures from that data;
3. keep suffix heuristics as prefill and last-resort fallback;
4. require term IDs or explicit `TermValue` data for deterministic runtime
   formatting.

That gives us the small-runtime benefits of the old prototype without depending
on its old data source or pretending that suffixes alone are enough for article,
plural, elision, or case-sensitive phrase rendering.

The first noun-focused Unicode report shows the size tradeoff:

| Pack shape | Coverage | Estimated size |
| --- | --- | ---: |
| old suffix/Bloom prototype | 42,295 unambiguous noun genders | 24.9 KB |
| Unicode-derived suffix/Bloom classifier | 16,590 single-token noun genders | 9.8 KB |
| Unicode-derived suffix-rule fixture | 16,590 single-token noun genders, 50 exported rules | 6.1 KB pretty JSON, 569 B exported rule bytes |
| Unicode noun V0 feature lower bound | 17,101 article-candidate surfaces | 188 KB |
| Unicode noun V0 simple sorted table | 17,101 article-candidate surfaces | 305 KB |
| Unicode noun V0 generated metadata rows | 17,101 article-candidate surfaces | 305 KB lower bound, 3.1 MB pretty JSON |
| Unicode noun V0 metadata + ambiguities | 17,101 exact surfaces + 135 ambiguous surfaces | 308 KB lower bound, 3.1 MB pretty JSON |

The Unicode table pack is larger because it stores explicit surface strings and
diagnostics, not just a gender classifier. That is acceptable for backend or
tooling, but likely too high for a default small web/client pack.

The Unicode-derived suffix/Bloom result is the interesting client fallback: it
reaches exact validation for its declared 16,590 single-token noun-gender set
after 10 correction entries, but it cannot prove that an arbitrary runtime word
is in the supported set. It is therefore a compact generated fallback and prefill
tool, not a replacement for term IDs, explicit metadata, or build-time
validation.

The client path should ship closed-world product terms for deterministic output
and optionally add the generated classifier for broad best-effort diagnostics.

Packaging implication for French V0:

1. Backend/authoring can load a fuller per-locale dictionary-derived metadata
   pack for prefill, diagnostics, and ambiguity surfacing.
2. Web/client runtime should default to the closed-world term pack for the
   product catalog being rendered.
3. A compact generated classifier can be an optional client add-on for
   best-effort hints, but it must not be the only source for deterministic
   grammar output.

## Dictionary-First Direction

Use the dictionary as the first production data source, not as an optional late
add-on. The runtime order should be:

1. explicit term form from Mojito;
2. explicit term metadata from Mojito;
3. compiled locale dictionary;
4. compact exception/correction tables;
5. suffix and phonology heuristics;
6. `unknown` or `ambiguous` diagnostic.

The local `unicode-org/inflection` checkout contains French dictionary source
resources under:

```text
inflection/resources/org/unicode/inflection/dictionary/
```

The relevant French files are:

- `dictionary_fr.lst`: Git LFS pointer, declared source size about 17.3 MB;
- `inflectional_fr.xml`: Git LFS pointer, declared source size about 1.2 MB;
- `supplemental_fr.lst`: tiny manually curated supplement with entries such as
  `hôtel: singular masculine noun vowel-start` and `Tour Eiffel: singular
  feminine noun`.

Those resources are covered by the repository license (`Unicode-3.0` in the
local checkout). If we use them, the generated artifact must record source,
license, upstream commit, generator command, and which source files contributed
to it.

The first materialized French dictionary report, pinned to upstream commit
`9436c9ad637722826f4d8f588c64f6b7efbc743a`, parsed:

| Metric | Count |
| --- | ---: |
| entries | 254,502 |
| unique surfaces | 254,136 |
| gender-tagged entries | 45,487 |
| ambiguous surfaces | 4,490 |
| surfaces with multiple genders | 395 |
| surfaces with multiple parts of speech | 3,207 |

The downloaded `dictionary_fr.lst` SHA-256 matched the Git LFS pointer:
`21e1a3d385db927d10d175a322ad72f55cac455ab6c960564c90fe6ac23fc53b`.
Examples such as `livre` and `tour` appear as single entries with both
masculine and feminine tags, which reinforces that bare surface lookup must be
allowed to return `ambiguous`.

This dictionary is likely good enough for the first real Mojito pass if we do
not ship it raw. Raw dictionary files are too large for a small client package,
but they are reasonable generator input. The compiled output should be a
French-only pack containing only the features we need first:

- normalized surface form;
- part of speech;
- gender;
- number or invariance;
- proper-noun marker;
- vowel-start/elision marker;
- optional plural or inflected forms where available;
- ambiguity marker when one surface has multiple grammatical readings.

For production formatting, the dictionary still should not override explicit
term forms. It should prefill term metadata, backstop known words, and power
diagnostics. Runtime rendering of product terms should remain deterministic for
term IDs.

## Core Model

Use a term-pattern-first model:

```text
message MF2 pattern
+ term-level MF2 form pattern
+ optional grammar metadata
+ locale profile
= formatted output
```

Messages stay small:

```mf2
Vous avez ramassé {$item :term article=definite}.
Vous avez trouvé {$count :count of=$item}.
```

The term owns localized forms:

```json
{
  "text": "épée",
  "forms": {
    "default": ".input {$usage :string}\n.input {$number :string}\n.input {$count :number}\n.match $usage $number $count\nbare singular * {{épée}}\ndefinite singular * {{l'épée}}\nindefinite singular * {{une épée}}\ncount * other {{{$count} épées}}\n* * * {{épée}}"
  },
  "morphology": {
    "partOfSpeech": "noun",
    "gender": "feminine",
    "number": "singular"
  }
}
```

Metadata remains useful for editor controls, validation, search, prefill,
fallbacks, diagnostics, and compact compilation. It should not be the only
runtime source of truth when the rendered form matters.

## Bare-Word Ambiguity

A bare word is not always enough to know the correct gender. French examples:

- `livre`: `le livre` means "the book", but `la livre` can mean "the pound".
- `tour`: `le tour` means "the turn/tour", but `la tour` means "the tower".

This does not block the design. Product localization usually deals with stable
terms or entities, so Mojito can store `item.book`, `unit.pound`, or
place-specific term IDs with their own localized forms and metadata.

The important API rule is that `genderOf("livre")` must not pretend to be
authoritative. It should return `ambiguous`, `unknown`, or a best-effort result
with source/confidence metadata unless the caller has supplied a term ID, sense,
domain, or declared supported dictionary.

Discussion point:

- Should Mojito require term IDs for production grammar formatting and reserve
  bare-word guessing for prefill/review tools, or should runtime formatting
  allow best-effort bare strings with diagnostics?

## French V0

For French, implement a small first pass:

- explicit term form wins;
- explicit term metadata wins over inference;
- dictionary lookup wins over suffix heuristics;
- count is passed as a normal MF2 dependency, for example `count=$count`;
- article/preposition rendering is phrase-level, not app string
  concatenation;
- heuristics prefill metadata and forms for translators, then diagnostics flag
  low-confidence or ambiguous cases.

Initial generated features:

- gender: masculine, feminine, ambiguous, unknown;
- number behavior: singular, plural, invariant, unknown;
- phonology: elides, does not elide, unknown;
- forms: bare singular/plural, definite, indefinite, count.

Initial heuristic sources:

- compiled dictionary entries with license/provenance metadata;
- clean-room suffix rules for common gender signals;
- regular plural rules with exception hooks;
- vowel/elision helper with explicit overrides;
- compact exception or correction tables generated from the dictionary.

## Build Small Blocks Or Rewrite Inflection

Do not start with a full rewrite of the Unicode inflection library. Start with
MF2-native blocks that are useful immediately and are shaped so they can grow
into a broader inflection runtime.

Small blocks to build first:

- dictionary-backed French term analyzer;
- term-form generator for article/count/elision rows;
- compiled term matcher for runtime rendering;
- structured diagnostics for missing, ambiguous, or heuristic-only data;
- Mojito term metadata storage and editor prefill.

This keeps the product path small and testable. A rewrite becomes justified only
after these blocks show that multiple locales need the same internal machinery:
dictionary compiler, compact feature tables, phrase renderer, and cross-runtime
pack readers. At that point we can lift the shared pieces into a standalone
inflection runtime without copying Unicode's entire architecture up front.

## Build-Time Expansion And Validation

Add a build-time grammar analyzer that expands MF2 term usages into concrete
locale requirements. This is the main mechanism that keeps runtime formatting
deterministic and keeps translators out of raw grammar plumbing.

Example message:

```mf2
Vous avez supprimé {$item :term article=definite count=$count}.
```

For French, the locale profile expands that usage into requirements for the
`item` argument:

```text
partOfSpeech=noun
gender=masculine|feminine|ambiguous-known
number behavior or plural form
vowel/elision behavior
forms for definite singular/plural
forms for count one/other where count is present
```

For a case language, a role-based message:

```mf2
Added {$item :term role=destination count=$count}.
```

can expand through the locale profile:

```text
de role=destination -> case=dative
ru role=object -> case=accusative + animacy-sensitive
```

The analyzer should emit a requirement manifest per message and locale:

```json
{
  "messageId": "inventory.deleted",
  "locale": "fr",
  "arguments": {
    "item": {
      "type": "term",
      "requires": [
        "partOfSpeech=noun",
        "gender",
        "plural",
        "elision",
        "forms.definite.singular",
        "forms.definite.plural"
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

Then the build checks every referenced term:

- explicit term forms satisfy the requirement;
- otherwise dictionary data can satisfy the requirement;
- otherwise generated heuristics may prefill but should leave a diagnostic;
- production builds can decide whether diagnostics are warnings or failures.

This should not blindly explode every possible combination. It should expand
only the finite selector axes that a message actually uses, normalized by the
locale profile: article usage, role/case, plural categories, animacy, elision,
and other supported dimensions. The output can also precompile term matchers
and emit synthetic fixture cases for the exact usage rows the app needs.

The ideal production contract is:

```text
message catalog + term catalog + locale profile -> no missing grammar metadata
```

If the build passes for a locale/package, runtime formatting for known terms
does not need to guess. Guessing remains for editor prefill, preview, and
diagnostics around unknown bare strings.

### Parser Adapter Contract

Mojito does not currently have a production Java MF2 parser in `common`. The
frontend has an `mf2-demo` extractor for protected-text experiments, and
`dev-docs/design/016-protected-text-editor.md` already calls that demo-only until
a parser with stable source spans exists.

The inflection analyzer should depend on the smaller contract it actually
needs, not on a specific parser implementation. The Java/common V0 surface
expresses that as `TermUsageExtractor`:

- return `:term` usages in source order;
- expose the argument name without the leading `$`;
- expose parsed key-value option strings, preserving variable references such
  as `$count`;
- expose a `SourceSpan` whose `start` and `end` are Java `String` UTF-16
  code-unit offsets covering the full term expression in the original message;
- reject impossible parser output, such as empty arguments or invalid spans.

`RegexTermUsageExtractor` is only the temporary implementation for the
experiment's tiny `{$arg :term key=value ...}` subset. It rejects bare options,
blank option values, invalid option keys, duplicate keys, and unbalanced quoted
values before semantic validation. The future real MF2 parser can replace it
without changing validation, report writing, or compiled-row rendering.

Parser implementation options checked so far:

- Mojito already has ICU4J in the parent dependency graph (`icu4j` 78.3).
- ICU4J 78.3 contains `com.ibm.icu.message2.MFParser`,
  `MessageFormatter`, and `MFDataModel`.
- The public data model exposes the semantic pieces the inflection analyzer
  wants: variable expressions, function references, function names, and option
  maps.
- The public data model does not expose stable source spans for expression
  nodes, so it does not fully satisfy `TermUsageExtractor` by itself.
- ICU documents the Java MF2 implementation as a technology preview that is
  still evolving. Treat ICU4J as the leading parser candidate, but do not make
  the inflection build depend on its tech-preview data model until the source
  span story is solved.

The first spike is `IcuMessage2TermUsageExtractor.java`. It uses ICU4J for MF2
semantic extraction, then pairs the semantic usages with a `TermUsageExtractor`
source-span side table; the default side table is still
`RegexTermUsageExtractor`. The tests prove ICU can recognize `:term` function
expressions in message bodies, `.input` declarations, and `.local` declarations,
including arguments, literal options, and variable-reference options for the
current fixture shape. It also recognizes `.match`/selector messages where the
`:term` usage is declared once before selection or repeated inside variant
bodies. The spike now sorts ICU option maps for deterministic report output and
fails explicitly if ICU semantic usages disagree with the span side table. This
is good enough to compare semantic behavior, but it still should not become the
default production analyzer until source locations are parser-backed rather than
regex-backed. If that remains fragile, implement the minimal common parser
subset needed for `:term` usage extraction and leave full formatting to ICU.

One ICU shape to avoid for now: `.input {$item :term count=$count}` together
with a separate `.input {$count :number}` is rejected as a duplicate variable
declaration. Until we understand whether that is intended MF2 validation or an
ICU tech-preview limitation, count-bearing `:term` usages should live in the
message or variant body, or the analyzer should model that shape explicitly.

## Closed-World And Open-World Terms

The build-time guarantee depends on whether the formatter knows the possible
term set before release.

Closed-world examples:

- app-owned product terms;
- glossary-backed terminology;
- game inventory or content catalogs included in the build;
- settings, navigation, and other product UI nouns.

For these, the build can validate every referenced term:

```text
message catalog + term catalog + locale profile -> exact requirement check
```

Open-world examples:

- user-generated names;
- external marketplace or plugin content;
- imported files;
- remote catalogs not known at build time;
- search results or other arbitrary runtime strings.

For these, the build can validate only the argument contract:

```text
message catalog + locale profile -> required runtime term shape
```

The runtime value then needs one of:

- a term ID that resolves to approved forms;
- a `TermValue` carrying explicit forms or morphology;
- a dictionary-analyzable string with diagnostics;
- an explicit fallback policy that accepts best-effort rendering.

Potential MF2 surface:

```mf2
{$item :term article=definite count=$count strict=term}
{$item :term article=definite count=$count fallback=best-effort}
```

Spec decision to track:

- Should `:term` default to strict closed-world validation and require
  open-world callers to opt into fallback, or should best-effort dictionary
  analysis be allowed by default with diagnostics? The prototype intentionally
  defaults to closed-world validation while this broader surface is unresolved.

## Runtime Shape

Use explicit resolved values:

```ts
type TermValue = {
  id: string;
  text: string;
  forms?: { default?: string | CompiledTermMatcher };
  morphology?: Record<string, unknown>;
};

type GrammarDiagnostic = {
  code: string;
  termId?: string;
  source: "explicit" | "heuristic" | "exception" | "unknown" | "ambiguous";
  message: string;
};
```

The formatter should require only key/value resource access:

```ts
type ResourceStore = {
  getString(key: string): string | undefined;
  getJson<T>(key: string): T | undefined;
  getBytes?(key: string): Uint8Array | undefined;
};
```

Authoring can use nested JSON. Runtime exports can use flat KV JSON, generated
arrays, or a binary/string-pool pack.

## Closed-World Term Pack

The deterministic runtime path should compile known product terminology into a
closed-world term pack. This is separate from the broad dictionary fallback.

Build inputs:

```text
MF2 message catalog
+ localized term catalog
+ locale grammar profile
+ optional dictionary-derived prefill data
= validated locale term pack
```

Example MF2 usage:

```mf2
Vous avez supprimé {$item :term article=definite count=$count}.
```

The build analyzer turns that into term requirements:

```json
{
  "messageId": "inventory.deleted",
  "locale": "fr",
  "argument": "item",
  "requires": [
    "partOfSpeech=noun",
    "gender",
    "number",
    "elision",
    "forms.definite.singular",
    "forms.definite.plural",
    "forms.count.one",
    "forms.count.other"
  ]
}
```

The authoring/export shape can stay readable:

```json
{
  "terms": {
    "item.iron_sword": {
      "text": "épée de fer",
      "morphology": {
        "partOfSpeech": "noun",
        "gender": "feminine",
        "number": "singular",
        "startsWithVowelSound": true
      },
      "forms": {
        "definite.singular": "l'épée de fer",
        "definite.plural": "les épées de fer",
        "count.one": "1 épée de fer",
        "count.other": "{$count} épées de fer"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

The runtime pack should be smaller and more mechanical:

```text
string pool:
  item.iron_sword, épée de fer, l'épée de fer, les épées de fer, ...

term rows:
  termIdIndex, defaultTextIndex, featureBits, formSetIndex

form rows:
  selectorKey(article=definite, number=singular) -> stringIndex
  selectorKey(article=definite, number=plural) -> stringIndex
  selectorKey(count=one) -> stringIndex
  selectorKey(count=other) -> patternIndex
```

For ambiguous surfaces, the term ID carries the sense:

```text
concept.book -> livre, masculine, "book"
unit.pound -> livre, feminine, "unit-pound"
```

At runtime, `:term` receives explicit function options and explicit
dependencies. It does not read a hidden global message context:

```text
formatTerm(termId=item.iron_sword, article=definite, count=$count)
```

Missing term IDs or missing required forms are build failures for closed-world
catalogs. Open-world `TermValue` inputs can use the same shape dynamically, but
must carry diagnostics or an explicit best-effort fallback policy.

The experiment scaffold contains a subset scanner,
`dev-docs/experiments/mf2-inflection/mf2_term_requirements.py`, plus French
usage and term-pack fixtures. It is not a production MF2 parser; it exists to
exercise the build-time contract before wiring this into the real parser/model.
`dev-docs/experiments/mf2-inflection/mf2_term_pack_compile.py` then compiles the
validated readable pack into a row-oriented string-pool shape for size and data
model checks. `mf2_term_pack_render.py` renders the same compiled rows for the
tiny French subset, including the `le livre` versus `la livre` term-ID split.
Run `dev-docs/experiments/mf2-inflection/run_smoke.py` before changing the
experiment pipeline.

The Java/common V0 implementation mirrors that compiled-row renderer through a
top-level `CompiledTermPack` data model consumed by the behavior-only
`common/src/main/java/com/box/l10n/mojito/mf2/inflection/CompiledTermPackRenderer.java`.
The concrete compiled-pack renderer, Hindi pronoun renderer, metadata index,
and generated locale-data audit/report loaders are package-private
implementation classes; product and REST callers enter through `Mf2TermRenderer`
and the schema-gated pack/manifest loaders.
`TermRequirementJsonLoader.java` reads the same readable usage and term-pack
JSON shapes as the Python experiment. `TermUsageExtractor.java` is the parser
adapter point for source-to-usage extraction. The current implementation is
`RegexTermUsageExtractor.java`, with `IcuMessage2TermUsageExtractor.java` as the
ICU-backed semantic spike. Both remain temporary around source spans; production
should replace them with the real MF2 parser once Mojito has one with stable
source spans. `TermRequirementValidator.java`
then mirrors the small Python scanner's build-time contract: it expands `:term`
usages into required morphology and form keys, validates readable term data
before row compilation, and reports missing metadata such as elision or missing
count forms. It defaults to closed-world validation, where a `:term` usage with
no bound term IDs is a diagnostic, and exposes an explicit open-world mode for
future dynamic `TermValue` callers. Diagnostics include the same source span as
the corresponding `:term` usage so editor/review surfaces can jump directly to
the failing expression. `TermRequirementReportJsonWriter.java` emits
the same report schema as `mf2_term_requirements.py` so the Java path can be
compared against the
experiment output. The Python scanner now enforces the same current option
subset (`article`, `case`, `count`) as the Java validator, so generated fixtures cannot
silently contain unsupported `:term` usage. The common tests include a
checked-in report fixture copied
from the Python path to lock Java/Python report parity, and run that same
fixture through the ICU-backed extractor to prove the parser spike can drive the
full requirement-report pipeline for the current message subset. A separate
selector-message report test covers `.match` catalogs where the same `:term`
requirement appears in multiple variants.
`CompiledTermPackJsonLoader.java` reads the generated compiled JSON shape into
the row model. This is intentionally still a readable-data validator, report
writer, row renderer, and JSON bridge, not a full MF2 runtime. The compiled
loader now retains term rows and validates generated string indexes, duplicate
term form sets, duplicate form keys, term-row/form-set consistency, and
non-empty compiled-pack diagnostics so generator mistakes fail before rendering.
The compiled-pack record also rejects orphan form sets without matching term
rows, preventing direct render calls from using rows that binding preflight and
pack metadata do not recognize.
It also validates the optional generated explicit-form `generationSummary`
sidecar when present. The full debug object stays outside the public runtime
model, but its validated `exportPolicy` is retained on `CompiledTermPack` so
packaging/API callers can inspect whether the pack is explicit-form-only,
compact-composed, review-gated, or blocked. The check pins exported/candidate
term counts, form-row counts, required-vs-missing form counts, per-term missing
cells, required export-policy metadata, export-policy reason-count totals,
exported form-key to compiled-row alignment, source-row maps, review diagnostics for the shared
`missing-form-cell` diagnostic reason, and diagnostic term IDs. This makes
Arabic, Hebrew, Malayalam, Swedish, and Danish generated-review metadata fail
fast if the generator drifts from the compiled rows.
It also preserves the runtime pack schema, locale, source provenance, and size
estimates, including binary lower-bound arithmetic and per-part
string-pool/term-row/form-row checks for compiled packs, so generated packs
cannot quietly lose their source/license or packaging metadata at the Java
boundary.
Numeric pack fields such as string indexes, feature bits, size estimates, and
source byte sizes must be JSON integral numbers; loaders reject fractional
values instead of truncating them.
Requirement expansion and rendering share the same option validator for the
current subset (`article`, `case`, `count`), and the temporary regex extractor rejects
malformed option syntax before semantic validation. The renderer rejects
unsupported `:term` options, unsupported article values, missing term bindings,
missing/non-numeric count variables, and missing pattern variables instead of
falling back to a plausible but wrong form. Its tests prove the same French
examples as the Python smoke pipeline, including `livre` split by term ID into
book and unit senses. The renderer now also has the first compact generated
form path: for Spanish article usage without case, explicit form rows still win,
but if they are absent the runtime composes definite/indefinite articles from
term-row gender, selected number, and the dictionary-derived `stressed` bit, then
renders the `bare.singular` or `bare.plural` form. Java and Python smoke
fixtures cover the stressed feminine override (`el agua`, `un agua`), regular
feminine forms (`la abeja`, `unas abejas`), explicit-form precedence, and
missing bare-form diagnostics. The checked-in Spanish compiled fixture is
generated from `es_term_usage_example.json` and `es_term_pack_example.json`,
preserves Unicode dictionary/inflectional source provenance, and is covered by
both JSON-loader rendering tests and mmap encode/decode rendering tests.
The Java DTO records defensively copy caller-provided lists/maps and reject
impossible spans, summary counts, and French metadata enum values; optional
generator/debug provenance remains nullable for small hand-written unit
fixtures, while source-backed runtime fixtures require a license, generator,
matching `sourceLabels`/`sources` entries, and nonblank unique source labels so
generated data remains traceable. Generated runtime fixtures also carry size
estimates.
`InflectionJsonFields` now starts consolidating the repeated generated-fixture
loader boilerplate: required and optional object/array fields, text/number/
boolean field checks, optional boolean fields, optional integer values/defaults,
nullable and required string-index bounds checks,
allowed string values, non-negative integer maps, positive export-policy reason
counts, text arrays, and immutable linked-map copies. Shared integer helpers now require JSON integral numbers, so
generated values such as string indexes, feature bits, byte sizes, and audit
counts cannot be silently truncated from decimals. The low-inflection audit
loader, French suffix-rule pack loader, French noun metadata pack loader, and
compiled-term pack JSON loader are the first migrated loaders, with direct
helper tests plus artifact tests for schema, source state, recommendation, row
metadata, provenance coherence, and compiled runtime-pack rendering/binary
round trips. The Norwegian Bokmal noun metadata loader now also uses the shared
helper for object/array/text/boolean/numeric fields, string indexes, text arrays,
feature-count maps, immutable map copies, and null object-mapper validation. The
Dutch noun metadata loader uses the same shared validation path for gender,
number, and diminutive metadata, including integral-number checks for row fields,
source byte sizes, and feature-count maps. The French suffix-rule and noun
metadata loaders now schema-gate generated fixtures instead of accepting
arbitrary JSON with the right top-level arrays. The term inflection profile-pack
loader now uses the same helper for pack/provenance/profile fields while keeping
its per-profile missing-provenance behavior explicit as an empty JSON object.
The Serbian and Russian case-form loaders now use the helper while preserving
their permissive textual string-pool loaders and locale-specific closed-key/form
row checks; the Hindi pronoun agreement loader uses the same helper while
keeping its selector-expansion and dependency/register rules local. String
indexes, skipped-term counts, summary counts, pronoun row line numbers, and
source byte sizes now share the integral-number guard used by compiled runtime
packs. The Serbian case-form loader now also rejects source-label/source
mismatches and recomputes string-pool, binary lower-bound, and JSON byte
estimates at load time; its summary now preserves `skippedTerms` as an
immutable, bounded reason-count sidecar so generator policy drift cannot hide in
unused JSON, and its `toCompiledTermPack()` path now carries locale, source
provenance, and binary lower-bound estimates into the shared compiled-pack
model. `GermanArticleCaseReportJsonLoader` is now the first generated
report loader on the shared path, including direct map-entry integer/text
parsing for feature maps and form maps. The Spanish, Italian, and Portuguese
noun report loaders now follow the same shared parsing path for
sources, provenance labels, feature maps, phrase-form maps, sample arrays, and
row/count estimates while preserving their article/agreement strategy checks
locally. The Russian
case-pack audit and Serbian case-pack report loaders now also use the
shared parsing path for sources, provenance labels, feature maps, numeric-key
feature maps, sample arrays, and row/count estimates while preserving
case-form conflict arithmetic and simple case-pack estimate checks locally. The
Arabic audit loader now follows the same shared parsing path for provenance,
feature maps, numeric-key pattern stats, pronoun samples, policy arrays, and
sample entries while preserving Arabic pack-policy, dual/construct, and pronoun
inventory checks locally. The Hebrew audit loader now pins the same
generator-side contract for construct-state data, including provenance,
feature maps, pattern slot coherence, pronoun inventory counts, sample bounds,
and the closed-world/no-noun-case V0 policy. The Malayalam audit loader now
pins the broad explicit-case contract, including provenance, case/gender
coverage arithmetic, pattern slot coherence, pronoun inventory counts, sample
bounds, and the closed-world case/number V0 policy before any suffix
composition work. The Germanic/Nordic group audit loader now pins the
Danish/Swedish explicit genitive-definiteness path against the Bokmal/Dutch
metadata-first path, including exact locale order, provenance hashes, pattern
slot coherence, sparse-case evidence, diminutive evidence, and pronoun inventory
shape. The Turkish and Hindi survey loaders now also use the
shared parsing path for provenance, source sizes, feature maps, numeric-key
pattern stats, sample arrays, and pack-shape counts while preserving
Turkish suffix slot validation and Hindi pronoun dependency-feature validation
locally. At this checkpoint, generated locale audit/report/survey loaders no
longer carry private field-reader copies. `TermRequirementJsonLoader` now also
uses the shared object, boolean, and immutable-map helpers while keeping its
intentionally permissive message/form text parsing local. All generated MF2
inflection JSON loader `load(String json)` entry points now reject null JSON
with a stable `json` parameter error before parsing. The runtime/tooling public
loader `load(JsonNode root)` entry points for compiled packs, binding
manifests, readable term packs, glossary profile packs, and the Hindi pronoun
pack also reject null roots with a stable `root` parameter error and non-object
roots with a stable artifact-labeled object-root error. The
deterministic report writers reject null `ObjectMapper` and report inputs with
stable `objectMapper`/`report` parameter errors. A package-level API contract
and source-scanning guard test now pin the public runtime/tooling surface
separately from package-private `@GeneratorSupport` and renderer implementation
classes. The package API-surface guard also pins that Java/common inflection has
no public CLI/main entry point; release validation remains an API-only boundary
covered by the shared MF2 conformance wrapper. The broad `common` MF2
inflection package test passes 698 tests.
`Mf2TermRenderer` now validates its public facade inputs, including runtime map
keys and values, before manifest/render work, and Hindi pronoun/agreement
rendering failures use the same argument, bound term, and source-span
diagnostic envelope as compiled-term failures. Default runtime and validation
entry points now use ICU MF2 term extraction, backed by a source scanner for
offsets, with explicit parity tests for the supported body, `.input`, `.local`,
and `.match` variant shapes. Both extractor paths reject null messages with a
stable `message` parameter error. The lightweight English/Korean
low-inflection path now pins real pronoun CSV metadata in Java before any
dictionary materialization decision. A package-level generated-fixture
provenance audit now covers the checked-in inflection fixtures, including
schema IDs, accepted licenses, generator paths, source-label/source-row count
coherence, source hashes/sizes, 64-character lowercase SHA-256 digest shape,
and low-inflection per-locale LFS/source
metadata. The release-package validation facade now consumes compiled JSON
packs, M2IF binary packs, and the Hindi pronoun sidecar and produces a stable
structured preflight report for product/build pipelines. It also accepts a
manifest-backed entry point with relative artifact paths under an explicit base
directory plus real-path containment checks, so build jobs can validate release
bundles without constructing Java objects by hand and without following
symlinked artifact paths outside the bundle. A Python/native conformance script
now mirrors the same
manifest/report contract in the experiment smoke pipeline, and a fixture-bundle
materializer turns the checked JSON plus `.m2if.hex` resources into a
35-artifact release-style bundle after validating artifact count, source paths,
target paths, kinds, and suffixes. The shared MF2 conformance gate now runs
`mf2/conformance/validate_inflection_release_fixture.py`, which materializes
that bundle, validates every artifact, and is wired into `mf2/check.sh`.
The Python MF2 package gate now exposes the same check as
`sh run.sh inflection-release`, keeping the inflection release contract outside
the `mojito_mf2` public API while still giving a standalone non-Java MF2
package an explicit conformance target without making it an inflection runtime.
The binary/mmap codec now also rejects
unsupported flags, misaligned
or overlapping sections, and trailing bytes, while preserving decode support
for valid positioned `ByteBuffer` slices and an explicit sidecar provenance
decode overload, plus an explicit embedded-metadata encode path that round-trips
source provenance while rejecting mismatched sidecars. A pinned Portuguese
hex-encoded `.m2if` fixture now locks the byte-level header, section directory,
embedded metadata, SHA-256, deterministic re-encode, and render behavior for
cross-runtime decoder work. Serbian case-form coverage now exercises the sidecar path end-to-end: Unicode-derived JSON pack ->
compiled rows -> binary encode -> decode with provenance -> render. Bound-message rendering now also performs a compiled-pack
preflight before runtime rendering, catching missing static form alternatives
such as a plural bare form required by `count=$count` even when a preview sample
uses the singular path. Manifest render preflight failures now carry the message
ID in that same argument, bound term, and source-span envelope. The public
facade now also exposes `requireRenderableBoundMessage` as a validation-only
entry point for REST and product callers that need to block on static
renderability without supplying sample runtime variables. The compiled-pack
rows, provenance, and size records now sit behind the top-level
`CompiledTermPack` model, and the concrete renderers/metadata index are now
package-private so production imports stay on `Mf2TermRenderer`. Generated
locale-data audit/report loaders are also package-private fixture bridges, not
product APIs.
Reported source spans now use the shared `SourceSpan` value object, which pins
diagnostic offsets to Java `String` UTF-16 code-unit indexes. The spans still
come from the prototype regex parser or ICU span side table until the real MF2
parser emits equivalent source locations directly.

`FrenchNounMetadataPackJsonLoader.java` is the first Java bridge for
Unicode-derived noun metadata packs. It reads the generated string-pool row
shape from `fr_noun_pack_report.py`, validates surface indexes, duplicate
surfaces, ambiguity rows, summary counts, and feature-bit/debug-field
consistency, preserves provenance metadata, then exposes rows as noun morphology
for editor prefill and diagnostics. It deliberately does not render forms;
deterministic production formatting still goes through explicit term IDs and the
term-form pack. Its tests include a tiny Python-generated fixture from the full
Unicode French dictionary, not only hand-written JSON. The fixture refresh
command lives in `dev-docs/experiments/mf2-inflection/README.md`.

`FrenchNounMetadataAnalyzer.java` adds the first lookup layer on top of that
pack. It normalizes bare French noun input the same way as the dictionary
parser, including trimming, lowercasing, and smart-apostrophe replacement, then
returns `EXACT` with noun morphology, `AMBIGUOUS` with multiple dictionary
analyses, or `UNKNOWN`. This keeps dictionary lookup conservative: it can
prefill metadata and power diagnostics, but it does not guess when the surface
is absent.

`FrenchGenderSuffixRulePackJsonLoader.java` reads the generated suffix-rule
fallback fixture from the same Unicode-derived noun data and preserves the same
license/source provenance block. When supplied to the analyzer, suffix matches
are returned as `HEURISTIC` with `SUFFIX_RULE` source, gender, and confidence.
Exact dictionary rows still win. This mirrors the useful part of the old French
heuristic prototype while making the source and confidence explicit enough for
diagnostics. Its tests also load a tiny Python-generated suffix-rule fixture.
Refresh it with the same command documented in the experiment README.

## Earlier Prototype Checkpoint

The initial prototype checkpoint was useful enough to evaluate the native-MF2
direction before the Java/common V0 hardening work. It had:

- a Python generator for Unicode-derived French noun metadata, ambiguity rows,
  suffix-rule fallback fixtures, source/license provenance, and size reports;
- checked-in generated Java fixtures that prove the generator output shape is
  loadable from `common`;
- Java loaders for closed-world term packs, French noun metadata packs, and
  French suffix-rule fallback packs;
- a Java analyzer that returns `EXACT`, `AMBIGUOUS`, `HEURISTIC`, or `UNKNOWN`
  with source/confidence metadata;
- Java build-time requirement validation for the current `:term` subset,
  including ICU4J semantic extraction for body, `.input`, `.local`, and
  `.match` message shapes;
- Java and Python renderer support for explicit case-form selection such as
  `case=accusative count=$count`, using closed-world compiled term forms rather
  than runtime dictionary inference;
- a generator-side Serbian case-form sample pack derived from Unicode
  nominative singular entries and `inflectional_sr.xml` templates, with Java
  loader coverage that converts the generated rows into the existing compiled
  renderer shape;
- a compiled Serbian case-form export in the shared `compiled-term-pack/v0`
  row shape, so the runtime path can use the existing generic term-pack loader
  instead of a Serbian-specific debug loader, while preserving source
  provenance and rough binary size estimates;
- a Java binary codec spike for the proposed mmap compiled-pack layout, with
  deterministic encode/decode, section-bound validation, strict UTF-8 string
  validation, row-width checks, and generated Serbian fixture round-tripping;
- defensive Java DTO boundaries for generated packs, requirement reports, and
  term usage records;
- Python and Java fixture parity for requirement reports and term rendering.

Still experimental:

- source spans still depend on a regex side table because ICU4J MF2 does not
  expose stable expression spans;
- dictionary, audit, and survey packs are still JSON/debug shaped. The shared
  compiled term pack has a binary/mmap codec spike, but the locale dictionary
  sidecars do not yet have a final production file format;
- suffix fallback and metadata classifiers are useful for prefill, review, and
  diagnostics, but they are not a production policy for guessing arbitrary bare
  words at render time;
- Mojito storage, REST, frontend review, Workbench binding, render preview, and
  MCP review paths now exist as first checkpoints, but the production review
  workflow and per-locale export policy still need hardening;
- runtime fixtures now cover multiple grammar families, but each locale still
  needs a production data decision: closed-world explicit forms, compact
  metadata composition, suffix composition, profile-only/no-op, or materialize
  more Unicode data.

Decision this supports: continue with small MF2-native blocks. Do not rewrite
the full Unicode inflection runtime yet; extract reusable pack generation,
lookup, diagnostics, and term-form rendering pieces only after the French path
proves useful in Mojito authoring and build validation.

### Other Locale Survey

The local Unicode inflection checkout declares locale groups for 25 language
families in `supported-locales.properties`: `ar`, `da`, `de`, `en`, `es`, `fr`,
`he`, `hi`, `id`, `it`, `ja`, `ko`, `ml`, `ms`, `nb`, `nl`, `pt`, `ru`, `sr`,
`sv`, `th`, `tr`, `vi`, `yue`, and `zh`. It has dictionary files for most of
those, but most are Git LFS pointers in the checkout. The immediately
materialized non-French case-language data is Serbian:

| Locale | Local data state | Relevant first categories | MF2/native implication |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `es` | dictionary/inflectional XML are LFS pointers, 37 MB + 4.1 MB upstream | gender, number, definiteness; pronoun cases exist but noun UI path is close to French | Best Romance follow-up once data is materialized; reuse French gender/article/count shape with Spanish article forms and plural rules. |
| `it` | materialized in local cache; checkout files remain LFS pointers, 27 MB + 1.4 MB upstream | gender, number, definiteness, vowel/elision class, masculine article class | Same pack family as French/Spanish, but article generation needs `il/i` vs `lo/gli` and `l'` handling, not only gender/number. |
| `pt` | materialized in local cache; checkout files remain LFS pointers, 2.3 MB + 638 KB upstream | gender, number, definiteness, preposition+article contractions, possessive/demonstrative agreement, pluralization | Smaller Romance data point in Unicode's default language bundle; best next compact-pack iteration. |
| `de` | materialized in local cache; checkout files remain LFS pointers | case, gender, number, definiteness | First real article/case agreement target: definite/indefinite forms need case and gender, not just count. |
| `da` | materialized in local cache; checkout files remain LFS pointers, 38.6 MB + 2.1 MB upstream | nominative/genitive, common/neuter, number, definiteness | First Danish genitive/definiteness runtime fixture exists; use it with Swedish to harden the Nordic explicit-form path before compact article/definiteness composition. |
| `nb` | materialized in local cache; checkout files remain LFS pointers, 9.5 MB + 249 KB upstream | masculine/feminine/neuter, number, definiteness; almost no noun case | First metadata-first validation fixture exists: useful gender/number/definiteness table, but no broad case runtime in V0. |
| `nl` | materialized in local cache; checkout files remain LFS pointers, 781 KB + 2.4 MB upstream | gender, number, sparse case, diminutive metadata | First metadata/diminutive validation fixture exists; noun case/definiteness data is too sparse for a broad V0 case pack. |
| `sv` | materialized in local cache; checkout files remain LFS pointers, 19.4 MB + 856 KB upstream | nominative/genitive, common/neuter, number, definiteness | First Swedish genitive/definiteness runtime fixture exists; pair it with Danish to harden the explicit-form path. |
| `sr` | materialized dictionary 80 KB and inflectional XML 122 KB | case, number, gender | Best immediate non-French experiment because data is local; prototype a case-form pack keyed by term ID plus `case`, `number`, `gender`. |
| `ru` | materialized in local cache; checkout files remain LFS pointers, 76 MB + 1.5 MB upstream | case, gender, number, animacy, preposition variants | Larger Slavic target after Serbian; adds animacy-sensitive accusative behavior and alternate-form policy questions. |
| `tr` | materialized in local cache; checkout dictionary/XML remain LFS pointers, supplemental file is real | case suffixes, plural suffixes, vowel harmony, consonant alternation, foreign/exception metadata | Different runtime family: rule-plus-exception suffix composition rather than eager article/case phrase tables. |
| `hi` | materialized in local cache; checkout dictionary/XML remain LFS pointers, pronoun CSV is real | direct/oblique/vocative case, gender, number, animacy, pronoun genitive dependency agreement | Default Unicode Inflection language and good next target: exact case-form rows are modest, but pronoun agreement depends on referenced noun gender/number. |
| `ar` | materialized in local cache; checkout files remain LFS pointers, 894 KB + 1.6 MB upstream | case, number including dual, definiteness/construct, gender, human/nonhuman, pronoun case rows | Next new grammar-family target; start with a generator audit for dual/construct/case/gender plus pronoun-attachment policy before production formatting. |
| `he` | materialized in local cache; checkout files remain LFS pointers, 9.7 MB + 19 MB upstream | number including dual, definiteness/construct, gender | First construct-state runtime fixture exists; no broad noun-case mode in V0. |
| `en` | checkout dictionary and inflectional XML remain LFS pointers, 5.1 MB + 635 KB upstream; pronoun CSV is real 1.5 KB | pronoun person/number/gender/case, vowel/consonant start helper data, likely plural/possessive term forms | Materialize only for a concrete English-source preview or TTS/SSML/product-copy use case; otherwise English is mostly a source-locale validation/profile target, not a blocking runtime inflection target. |
| `ko` | checkout dictionary remains a 19 KB LFS pointer; no `inflectional_ko.xml` is present in the local dictionary directory; pronoun CSV is real 2.2 KB | formal/casual pronoun rows, gendered third-person pronouns, likely low noun inflection | Treat as pronoun/profile metadata first unless product copy needs Korean-specific binding or politeness handling; dictionary materialization is cheap but not yet tied to a pack shape. |

Unicode's own Stage 1 scope suggested English, Spanish, Serbian, German, and
French as a balanced first set. For this Mojito prototype, the first useful
slices are now covered across French, Serbian, German, Spanish, Italian,
Portuguese, Russian, Turkish, Hindi, Arabic, Hebrew, Malayalam, Swedish, and
Danish, with Norwegian Bokmål and Dutch covered by metadata-validation
fixtures. The
Portuguese compact checkpoint covers
articles and the highest value preposition contractions (`o/a/os/as`,
`um/uma`, `do/da`, `no/na`, `pelo/pela`) while keeping possessives and
demonstratives in the generated report/data-model track. Russian covers the
larger Slavic case/gender/number/animacy stress test with explicit
unambiguous-row policy. Turkish is the first suffix-composition target: the
default noun pattern has no explicit XML rows, so the pack should infer ordinary
vowel harmony/consonant metadata from the term surface, use supplemental
exception rows for foreign or irregular terms, keep explicit XML templates for
the small irregular set, and render the first compact plural/case suffix forms
from bare singular terms plus suffix metadata. Polish is not present in the
pinned local Unicode Inflection checkout used for this survey
(`9436c9a`, 2026-05-19): `supported-locales.properties` has no `locale.group.pl`,
the dictionary directory has no `dictionary_pl.lst` or `inflectional_pl.xml`,
and the local materialized cache has no Polish data. This is a source-data
availability statement for the current investigation, not a claim that Polish
inflection should be out of scope forever.

`locale_data_survey.py` now records this availability mechanically. The current
survey fixture covers 25 locale groups, 24 dictionary files, 17 inflectional XML
files, 14 cache-materialized dictionary/XML pairs, one checkout-materialized
pair (`sr`), and pronoun CSV files for every supported locale group. Fourteen
locales already have checked Java/common runtime fixtures (`ar`, `da`, `de`,
`es`, `fr`, `he`, `hi`, `it`, `ml`, `pt`, `ru`, `sr`, `sv`, `tr`).
`low_inflection_locale_audit.py` now covers the remaining low-inflection groups:
`en` and `ko` are `data-materialization-required` because their dictionary or
inflectional sources are still Git LFS pointers, while `id`, `ja`, `ms`, `th`,
`vi`, `zh`, and `yue` are `profile-only-noop` term-rendering locales with
pronoun inventory/profile metadata only. The Java fixture tests now pin the
real English pronoun case/person/number/gender counts, Korean
case/person/number/gender plus formal/casual register counts, and the derived
profile/no-op pronoun metadata pack. The generated profile pack covers 9
locales, 162 pronoun rows, 137 unique pronoun values, materialized pronoun CSV
source hashes, and `runtimeTermInflection=false`.

### Production Data Gap Matrix

This matrix is the working handoff from the Java/common V0 surface to production
locale-pack design. "Runtime status" means Java/common can already validate and
render at least one checked fixture through the shared MF2 `:term` path or, for
metadata-only locales, can validate the generated dictionary metadata.

| Locale family | Runtime status | Available Unicode-style data | Required grammar categories | Compact pack direction | Production gap |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `fr` | gender/article/count runtime plus dictionary and suffix metadata analyzers | Full dictionary materialization path documented; supplemental file is in checkout; generated fixtures carry Unicode provenance | gender, number, elision, article, ambiguity/sense | dictionary metadata for authoring plus closed-world compiled term forms; suffix rules only for prefill | Pin the full dictionary artifact in the normal cache/source flow and decide how glossary review approves ambiguous rows. |
| `es`, `it`, `pt` | compact article/agreement composition runtime | cached dictionary/XML for all three; report fixtures validate source hashes and article samples; all three now pin automatic/review/blocked compact-export policy counts | gender, number, article class or stress, optional Portuguese preposition contraction | compact gender/number/class metadata plus runtime shell composition; explicit forms override | Promote the report generators to reviewed glossary export with exception diagnostics and cross-runtime parity beyond the sample fixtures. |
| `de` | closed-world article/case runtime fixture | cached dictionary/XML plus contraction table and generated article/case report with automatic/review/blocked policy counts | case, gender, number, definiteness, article class | backend/build dictionary validation with client-shipped closed-world product forms | Decide whether any compact German article metadata ships to clients; ambiguity/skipped-row diagnostics are now pinned for authoring. |
| `sr`, `ru` | explicit case-form runtime fixtures | Serbian is checkout-materialized; Russian is cache-materialized; both have generator-side audits/reports with automatic/review/blocked policy counts | case, number, gender; Russian also animacy and variant policy | closed-world explicit form rows keyed by term ID and selector | Move the pinned skipped-row/variant policy from pretty JSON fixtures to production binary/mmap packaging and glossary approval UX. |
| `tr` | suffix-composition runtime plus explicit-template exception runtime | cached dictionary/XML and real supplemental file | plural, case suffixes, vowel harmony, consonant class, foreign/exception metadata | rule-safe suffix metadata for ordinary nouns plus explicit rows for XML mutation/irregular templates | Rule-safe eligibility is pinned to `inflection=1` with explicit-review flags; exception-review diagnostics surface in glossary approval, and approved product rows compile through glossary export. |
| `hi` | noun case runtime plus dependency-aware pronoun agreement runtime | cached dictionary/XML and real pronoun CSV | direct/oblique/vocative case, gender, number, second-person register, genitive dependency agreement | compiled noun case forms plus physically decoupled pronoun agreement table | Add production export/packaging for the pronoun table and harden cross-argument authoring UX. |
| `ar` | explicit case/number/construct runtime fixture | cached dictionary/XML and pronoun CSV; audit fixture validates dual/construct policy; compiled fixtures now pin review-required export policy for missing cells and a fully populated approved sample term; webapp export now pins fully populated approved Arabic product rows | case, number including dual, definiteness/construct, gender, animacy metadata | closed-world explicit forms with construct and dual as explicit keys | Keep product export review UX/MCP narrow; Arabic runtime and glossary export compilation accept reviewed product rows for the pinned construct/case/number samples, not open-world coverage. |
| `he`, `ml` | construct-state and multi-case runtime fixtures | cached dictionary/XML and generated audit/pack fixtures; Hebrew audit validation pins the no-case V0 policy and no dictionary-only fully populated approved candidate; Malayalam audit validation pins broad explicit-case V0 policy; compiled fixtures now pin review-required export policy for missing cells, an approved Malayalam fully populated term, Java/webapp-reviewed Hebrew product-row export, and webapp-reviewed Malayalam product-row export | Hebrew construct/definiteness/gender/number; Malayalam nominative/accusative/dative/genitive/locative/instrumental/sociative/vocative | closed-world explicit forms for the supported rows | Keep product export review UX/MCP narrow; Hebrew and Malayalam runtime plus glossary export compilation accept reviewed product rows for the pinned construct-dual and multi-case samples, not language-wide coverage. |
| `sv`, `da` | genitive/definiteness runtime fixtures | cached dictionary/XML and Java-validated Nordic audit fixtures | common/neuter, number, definiteness, nominative/genitive | explicit form rows today; possible compact definiteness composition later | Decide whether Nordic V0 stays explicit-only or adds compact article/definiteness composition. |
| `nb`, `nl` | metadata validation only | cached dictionary/XML, Java-validated Nordic audit fixture, and metadata fixtures | Bokmal gender/number/definiteness; Dutch gender/number/diminutive with sparse case/definiteness | authoring metadata and diagnostics, not a broad renderer yet | Choose if any runtime composition is worth implementing; otherwise keep as metadata/profile support. |
| `en`, `ko` | pronoun/profile audit plus profile-pack validation | pronoun CSVs are real; dictionary/XML sources still need materialization or are incomplete | pronoun person/number/gender/case; Korean register | profile data first; no runtime noun pack until product need is concrete | Do not materialize LFS dictionary sources just to chase completeness; wait for a source-preview, TTS/SSML, or product-copy requirement. |
| `id`, `ja`, `ms`, `th`, `vi`, `zh`, `yue` | profile-only/no-op audit plus profile-pack validation | pronoun CSVs only for the current MF2 term-rendering needs | pronoun inventory/profile metadata | no-op term renderer plus profile metadata | Keep out of runtime pack work unless product copy needs locale-specific term binding. |
| `pl` | unavailable in pinned local source checkout | no `locale.group.pl`, no `dictionary_pl.lst`, no `inflectional_pl.xml`, and no local cache data in the `9436c9a` Unicode Inflection checkout used for this survey | unknown from this data source | blocked | Track as a separate source-data acquisition question before implementation; use another authoritative source only after the licensing/provenance path is explicit. |

The Java/common V0 surface now has byte-level M2IF parity for every checked compiled
fixture, so the next phase is productionization by runtime family rather than
blind locale expansion. Each family should satisfy the following gate before it
is considered production-ready outside Java:

| Family | Data/source gate | Pack-shape gate | Validation gate | Cross-runtime gate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Romance (`es`, `it`, `pt`) | Cached Unicode dictionary/XML plus generated report provenance and export-policy counts stay stable | Compact article/gender/number metadata with explicit exception forms | Review/blocked diagnostics map cleanly into glossary approval | Add at least one non-Java parity render per article family beyond the pinned samples. |
| Germanic case (`de`) | Cached dictionary/XML and contraction table hashes stay stable | Decide explicit closed-world product forms vs compact article table before client shipping | Ambiguous/skipped article-case rows are reviewable by term | Keep pinned M2IF fixture as byte contract; add a second term if compact mode ships. |
| Slavic case (`sr`, `ru`) | Serbian checkout data and Russian cached data remain provenance-pinned | Closed-world explicit rows keyed by `case`, `number`, and gender/animacy metadata | Variant/animacy/skipped-row policy is visible in authoring diagnostics | Existing Serbian/Russian M2IF fixtures are the native decoder gate; add animacy-focused Russian parity if product needs accusative animate terms. |
| Turkish | Cached dictionary/XML plus supplemental exceptions stay stable | Rule-safe suffix metadata for ordinary nouns, explicit rows for XML irregulars | Eligibility for suffix composition is frozen to `inflection=1` plus explicit-review flags; rejected terms surface as glossary review diagnostics | Existing suffix/manual/automatic explicit-template M2IF fixtures are the native decoder gate; product-approved explicit rows are covered by glossary export tests rather than a separate generated approval fixture. |
| Hindi | Cached dictionary/XML and pronoun CSV stay stable | Noun case rows remain decoupled from the versioned `dependency-pronoun-agreement-rows-v0` JSON sidecar; no pronoun rows are embedded in `compiled-term-pack/v0` or M2IF V0 | Cross-argument genitive dependency diagnostics are authorable | Existing noun-case M2IF fixture plus standalone pronoun-table JSON parity are the decoder gates; binary pronoun packaging is a future separate sidecar, not an extension of the main term-pack codec. |
| Semitic/Indic explicit forms (`ar`, `he`, `ml`) | Cached data and audit fixtures preserve review-required evidence | Closed-world explicit rows with review-required missing cells plus fully populated approved rows where dictionary coverage allows | Missing-cell diagnostics round-trip through glossary/profile review, and compiled export-policy reason counts must match the distinct reviewed terms named by diagnostics | Existing M2IF fixtures pin first grammar axes; Arabic and Malayalam now have fully populated approved M2IF fixtures, while Hebrew audit evidence shows approved rollout needs product-reviewed rows rather than a dictionary-only fully populated sample. |
| Nordic (`sv`, `da`) | Cached data and group audit keep broad genitive/definiteness evidence | Stay explicit in V0 unless compact suffix/article composition proves small and deterministic | Common/neuter and definiteness ambiguity are visible to reviewers | Existing Danish/Swedish export-policy M2IF fixtures are the decoder gate. |
| Metadata/profile (`nb`, `nl`, `en`, `ko`, `id`, `ja`, `ms`, `th`, `vi`, `zh`, `yue`) | Do not materialize new LFS data without a product use case | Profile/no-op or metadata-only packs | Validate profile metadata and block unsupported runtime assumptions | No M2IF runtime fixture unless a real product message needs inflection. |

### Non-Java Runtime Target Selection

The Java/common V0 surface is now far enough along that the next runtime choice
should be driven by product surface, not by language coverage. The Python and
JavaScript `inflection-release` commands are package-level conformance/build
wrappers over the shared release-fixture gate only; they do not make
`mojito_mf2` or `@mojito-mf2/core` inflection runtimes and should not expose
public inflection classes or subpaths.

| Runtime | Product surface | First pack shape | First grammar slice | Validation gate | Decision |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| TypeScript/JavaScript | Workbench/editor preview and browser clients | Compiled JSON first; M2IF only if bundle size or mmap-style reads become necessary | Romance compact article metadata plus explicit-form families already covered by frontend parity | Existing Workbench/renderer tests, `@mojito-mf2/core` parser/formatter export plus `.d.ts` package-boundary non-leak tests, `npm run inflection-release`, and the shared 35-artifact release fixture | First real non-Java product target when client-side rendering needs to leave Mojito webapp utilities. |
| Python | build/conformance tooling | Manifest/report plus materialized JSON/M2IF fixtures | No production grammar runtime yet | `sh run.sh inflection-release` and package public API non-leak test | Keep as validation harness, not app runtime. |
| Standalone Java MF2 | Parser/formatter package parity, separate from Java/common inflection V0 | None until a caller needs term rendering outside common | No standalone inflection runtime; Java/common owns checked V0 | Conformance runner scans public Java declarations for separator-normalized inflection/M2IF/compiled-pack leaks | Keep parser/formatter-only; do not duplicate Java/common runtime APIs. |
| Kotlin/JVM | Parser/formatter package parity for JVM callers | None until a Kotlin caller needs term rendering | Explicit forms only if a real Kotlin product integration appears | Conformance runner scans public/default declarations for separator-normalized inflection/M2IF/compiled-pack leaks | Defer; current package mirrors MF2 parsing/formatting, not inflection. |
| Swift | mobile or embedded clients | JSON for first integration, M2IF when compact binary resources matter | Closed-world explicit forms before compact language-specific composition | Future mobile/embedded work should use the repo-level shared release fixture plus product-relevant JSON/M2IF render fixtures; README pins that no `MessageFormat2Inflection` product exists today | Start only for a concrete mobile/embedded client. |
| Rust | server, CLI, or Wasm performance path | M2IF reader can be justified if runtime memory/throughput matters | Explicit forms first; compact families after measured need | Future private decoder work should use the repo-level shared release fixture plus all 17 pinned M2IF byte fixtures; current root-export integration test pins no inflection/M2IF/compiled-pack public exports today | Good high-performance candidate, but not a default next step without a caller. |
| Go | service integration if a Go caller appears | JSON manifest before binary | Explicit forms only | Repo-level release fixture gate only; platform-formatting remains separate and deferred; conformance test pins no inflection/M2IF/compiled-pack exported names today | Defer; current Go MF2 package intentionally has no platform adapter and no product inflection consumer. |
| PHP | legacy/server integration | JSON only unless a real package-size constraint appears | Explicit forms only | Repo-level release fixture gate plus existing PHP conformance; conformance test pins no public inflection/M2IF/compiled-pack functions or classes today | Defer unless a PHP integration needs term rendering. |

Do not start a public non-Java inflection runtime by copying the Java classes
into another package. The entry bar is:

1. name the product caller and whether it needs browser, mobile, server, CLI,
   or Wasm rendering;
2. choose JSON-only or M2IF based on package-size, load-time, and resource
   constraints for that caller;
3. implement a private decoder/renderer first, with no root package export until
   the API shape is reviewed;
4. validate against the shared 35-artifact release fixture and at least one
   product-relevant render fixture;
5. add a package-boundary test proving inflection internals do not leak through
   the stable root API unless the runtime is intentionally promoted.

Current readiness conclusion: Java/common is the production-quality runtime
path for the checked MF2 inflection V0 slices, and every non-Java package
remains without a public inflection runtime until a concrete product caller
satisfies the entry bar above. JavaScript/TypeScript is the first likely
product runtime if browser or Workbench rendering needs to leave Mojito webapp
utilities; Rust is the performance candidate only when a server, CLI, or Wasm
caller needs M2IF reads.
Python and JavaScript keep package-local wrappers that delegate to the shared
35-artifact release gate; Swift, Go, PHP, standalone Java, and Kotlin should
stay on their existing conformance/native commands unless a mobile, service,
legacy, or JVM caller is named.

Scope boundary: this is not "all inflection types" and not all Unicode/CLDR
languages. The production-quality claim is limited to the checked Java/common
V0 runtime and validation slices listed below. Anything outside these slices is
either metadata/profile validation, explicit backlog, or blocked on a product
caller and source-data decision.

| Area | V0 implemented | Not implemented yet |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Locale runtime coverage | Java/common render validation for `ar`, `da`, `de`, `es`, `fr`, `he`, `hi`, `it`, `ml`, `pt`, `ru`, `sr`, `sv`, and `tr`; metadata/profile validation for `en`, `id`, `ja`, `ko`, `ms`, `nb`, `nl`, `th`, `vi`, `yue`, and `zh` | Runtime term renderers for metadata/profile-only locales; Polish or any locale absent from the current Unicode inflection checkout; arbitrary CLDR locale fallback. |
| Grammar categories | Noun/article/case/construct/definiteness/suffix/pronoun-dependency slices named in the fixture set | Full language grammars, verb/adjective/adverb inflection, broad pronoun systems beyond the Hindi sidecar and profile inventories, full possessive/demonstrative agreement, and phrase-level agreement outside the current `:term` contract. |
| Data model | Closed-world compiled term packs, explicit reviewed rows, compact Romance/Turkish metadata where deterministic, and source-backed metadata/profile reports | Open-world dictionary inflection at runtime, unchecked heuristic generation, dictionary-wide client shipping, and dictionary-only rollout for Hebrew rows that require product review. |
| Runtime surfaces | Java/common public API, Mojito glossary/profile export-review path, Workbench diagnostics/render surfacing, and shared release-validation fixtures | Public package-local inflection APIs for Python, JavaScript, Rust, Swift, Go, or PHP; non-Java runtime promotion without a named caller. |
| Cross-runtime contract | ID/kind/path-pinned 35-artifact release fixture gate and 17 M2IF byte fixtures as artifact/byte contracts | Full non-Java renderer parity for every grammar family; binary auxiliary sidecars for Hindi pronoun agreement or future dependency tables. |

Remaining inflection coverage backlog:

1. Product-reviewed explicit rows for languages where Unicode data is not
   complete enough to export automatically, especially Hebrew construct-dual
   and Semitic/Indic missing-cell review flows.
2. Within-language category expansion only after product messages require it:
   possessive and demonstrative agreement in Romance/Portuguese, broader
   pronoun handling, adjective agreement, and any verb-like morphology are out
   of V0.
3. Metadata/profile locales (`nb`, `nl`, `en`, `ko`, `id`, `ja`, `ms`, `th`,
   `vi`, `zh`, `yue`) need product-driven requirements before becoming runtime
   term-rendering locales.
4. English and Korean dictionary materialization stays blocked until a concrete
   source-preview, TTS/SSML, or product-copy use case justifies pulling the LFS
   dictionary data.
5. Missing languages such as Polish are source-data acquisition work, not
   implementation work in the pinned local Unicode Inflection checkout used for
   this survey.
6. Non-Java package runtimes remain private or absent until a browser, CLI,
   server, mobile, legacy, or build-tool caller names the load format and API
   boundary it needs.

The current locale-data and low-inflection audits regenerate without fixture
drift, and `pronoun_profile_pack.py` now turns the low-inflection audit into a
profile/no-op metadata contract before any English or Korean dictionary
materialization. English and Korean have real pronoun CSV inventories (`en`: 38
rows with nominative/accusative/genitive, person, number, and gender; `ko`: 48
rows with nominative/accusative/genitive, person, number, formal/casual
register, and gender), but the noun data is still unavailable or incomplete in
the local checkout. Indonesian, Japanese, Malay, Thai, Vietnamese, Chinese, and
Cantonese remain profile-only/no-op for term rendering unless a concrete product
message needs locale-specific term binding. The next decision is whether this
profile pack stays generator/test-only or becomes an exposed metadata artifact
for authoring, not whether these locales need runtime noun inflection today.

Decision: keep the profile/no-op pronoun metadata pack generator/test-only for
now. It validates source availability and profile metadata, but it does not
compile to `CompiledTermPack`, does not expose a public common API, and should
not be surfaced through REST/MCP until a concrete authoring workflow needs
pronoun-profile previews. `Mf2InflectionApiSurfaceTest` pins
`PronounProfilePackJsonLoader` as package-private `@GeneratorSupport` so the
boundary cannot drift accidentally.

Remaining-language sweep decision: no additional V0 runtime term renderer is
warranted for `nb`, `nl`, `en`, `ko`, `id`, `ja`, `ms`, `th`, `vi`, `zh`, or
`yue` before broader rollout. Norwegian Bokmal and Dutch stay metadata-only:
they validate source-backed authoring metadata and diagnostics, but they do not
compile to runtime form rows until a real message needs definiteness,
diminutive, or another locale-specific operation. English and Korean stay
profile-only/data-materialization-required: their pronoun inventories are
pinned, but noun data must not be materialized until a concrete source-preview,
TTS/SSML, or product-copy use case exists. Indonesian, Japanese, Malay, Thai,
Vietnamese, Chinese, and Cantonese stay profile-only/no-op because the current
checkout has pronoun CSVs but no noun dictionary or inflectional XML rows that
justify runtime term inflection.

Post-Java locale productionization should now proceed in this order:

1. `he`: first fixture and Java-validated audit exist. The data has rich
   gender/number and construct-state rows, but almost no noun case rows, so V0
   is a Hebrew-specific construct-state explicit-form pack rather than reuse of
   Arabic case handling. The compiled fixture now marks missing construct-dual
   cells through review-required export policy. The audit also pins the
   approved-fixture candidate search: zero clean noun/proper-noun groups have
   all five required bare/construct singular/plural/dual rows, one clean group
   has only a `construct.dual` row, and 181 near-complete groups still miss
   `construct.dual`. The common profile-pack loader now has a reviewed Hebrew
   product-row test that compiles and renders all five construct-state forms,
   so next Hebrew hardening is product-term review UX/MCP entry that can collect
   those rows, not open-world generation.
2. `ml`: review-required and approved fixtures plus a Java-validated audit exist. The 54 MB dictionary has
   broad explicit noun case coverage across nominative, accusative, dative,
   genitive, locative, instrumental, sociative, and vocative, while
   gender/animacy metadata is sparse. V0 starts with closed-world multi-case
   explicit-form fixtures, including `case=sociative` and an approved
   `case=vocative` path for `ml.case.father`, and defers suffix
   composition/pronoun profile work. The compiled fixture now marks missing
   vocative cells through review-required export policy.
3. `sv` and `da`: first Nordic fixtures exist. Swedish exports `bostad` and
   `chassi`; Danish exports `franskmand` and `barnebarn`. Both use bare
   singular/plural aliases plus explicit `indefinite|definite` x
   `nominative|genitive` x `singular|plural` rows, and Java validates JSON,
   binary/mmap, common/neuter metadata, rendering, and the compiled export
   policy that keeps V0 on explicit form rows while deferring article,
   definiteness-suffix, and genitive-suffix composition.
   `GermanicNordicPackAuditJsonLoader` now also validates the shared group
   audit behind that decision: Danish and Swedish must retain broad
   nominative/genitive plus definiteness evidence, while Bokmal and Dutch must
   stay on the metadata-first path unless their source counts materially
   change.
4. `nb`: first metadata-first fixture exists. It validates 114,535
   metadata-candidate rows across 114,089 surfaces, keeps masculine/feminine/
   neuter, singular/plural, and definite/indefinite as compact bitsets, and
   preserves review diagnostics for multi-number and multi-gender rows such as
   `barn` and `bøkene`. It is not a noun-case renderer.
5. `nl`: first metadata/diminutive fixture exists. It validates 5,312
   metadata-candidate rows across 5,308 surfaces, tracks 2,412
   metadata-backed diminutive rows, and keeps `diminutive` as a compact feature
   bit. It is not a case or definiteness renderer because only 80 term rows are
   case-tagged and only 12 are definiteness-tagged.
6. `en` and `ko`: do not build runtime term-inflection packs until their Git
   LFS dictionary/inflectional sources are materialized and tied to a product
   use case. English currently has dictionary and inflectional pointers; Korean
   has a dictionary pointer and no inflectional XML.
7. `id`, `ja`, `ms`, `th`, `vi`, `zh`, and `yue`: profile-only/no-op for MF2
   term rendering. The current checkout has no noun dictionary or inflectional
   XML rows that justify a runtime pack, but each locale has a pronoun inventory
   that can remain profile metadata.

The first Arabic audit report parses 11,023 dictionary entries, 1,629
noun/proper-noun term entries, 6,080 noun/proper-noun/adjective agreement
entries, 130 nominative singular base candidates, 1,601 dual agreement entries,
412 construct agreement entries, 3,841 fully specified
case/number/gender/definiteness agreement rows, 604 XML patterns, no missing
agreement patterns, 1,222 ambiguous agreement surfaces, and 52 pronoun rows.
`ArabicPackAuditJsonLoader` now validates that generated audit fixture contract
in Java with the shared JSON-field helper for strict integral-number and
text-array parsing, plus local non-negative count checks for the Arabic audit
records. The audit fixture now also carries the Arabic V0 `packPolicy`: use a
closed-world explicit-form pack for noun/proper-noun terms, expose `case`,
`number`, and `definiteness` as runtime form keys, keep `gender`, `animacy`, and
part of speech as metadata, model dual via an explicit `number=dual` option
rather than implicit count mapping, model construct as an explicit form key,
keep adjective agreement out of V0, and treat the pronoun CSV as inventory only
until a separate Arabic attachment profile exists. The audit policy also pins
quantitative `reviewRequiredEvidence`: 1,222 ambiguous surfaces, 412 construct
rows, 1,601 dual rows, 18 verb-bearing term-pattern slots, 891
missing-or-ambiguous gender rows, and 52 pronoun rows; Java validation and the
Python smoke pipeline now regenerate and compare the checked-in audit fixture.
